repairBuyOrReplace.h2: E‑Class E200 CDI / E200d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential maintenance — catch it before catastrophic failure
Piston‑valve contact means little is left to repair
Essential maintenance — catch before injectors seize

buyingChecks.title: Buying This E200 CDI / E200d? Check These First
buyIf: * W213 (2016+) with the OM654 engine (most refined and efficient)
* W212 with documented timing chain replacement or inspection
* W211 with injector seals recently replaced (no "black death")
* Full service history with regular oil changes (critical for chain longevity)
* No cold‑start rattling (timing chain health — OM651 particularly)
* Evidence of regular longer runs (DPF health)
avoidIf: * OM651 (W212) with cold‑start rattle and no chain history — assume it's overdue
* Black carbon deposits around injectors — "black death" is developing
* No service history — all three engines are sensitive to oil quality
* Short‑journey ownership history — DPF and EGR issues likely
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — may indicate timing, injector, EGR or sensor problems

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The E200 CDI / E200d is Mercedes‑Benz's entry‑level diesel saloon and estate across three generations. The W211 (2002–2009) uses the OM646 — a robust engine with a weakness for injector seal leaks ("black death") and crankshaft sensor failure. The W212 (2009–2016) uses the OM651 — a 2.2‑litre engine with 136 bhp and 360 Nm torque, but with a well‑documented single timing chain that can stretch. The W213 (2016–present) uses the OM654 — Mercedes' fourth‑generation 2.0‑litre diesel producing 150 bhp, lighter and more efficient than the OM651, but with its own set of issues including timing chain wear, injector seal leaks, and EGR/DPF problems. With typical E‑Class values ranging from £4,000 (W211) to £25,000+ (W213), an engine replacement is a significant investment — a well‑maintained W213 OM654 with full service history is the safest choice.

cta.label: → Compare E200 CDI / E200d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class E200 CDI / E200d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or clattering for 2–5 seconds (timing chain tensioner warning — OM651) — see the map above
* Injector Seals — Inspect for black carbon deposits around injectors ("black death" warning — OM646/OM651)
* Service History — Including which engine era (OM646, OM651 or OM654) and oil‑change discipline
* Timing Chain — For OM651 (W212): check for any chain work or cold‑start rattle
* Crankshaft Sensor — Check for intermittent starting issues (OM646 — common failure)
* EGR System — Check for any loss of power or warning lights; EGR clogging is common
* DPF Regeneration — Check history; short journeys accelerate wear on all diesel eras
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate timing, injector, EGR or sensor problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures

items.question: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz E200 CDI / E200d engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned E200 CDI engine typically costs £3,000–£5,500 supply only, with the OM654 variant at the higher end. Fitting adds £800–£1,300 depending on the specialist. A replacement OM651.925 engine costs on average £3,074, with new or reconditioned units up to £4,753. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your E‑Class.

items.question: Is the Mercedes‑Benz E200 CDI / E200d engine reliable?
items.answer: It depends on which engine era your E‑Class has. The W211 (2002–2009) with the OM646 is robust but suffers from injector seal leaks ("black death") and crankshaft sensor failure. The W212 (2009–2016) with the OM651 has a well‑documented single timing chain that can stretch — a critical weakness. The W213 (2016–present) with the OM654 is lighter, more efficient and quieter, but owners have reported timing chain wear, injector seal leaks, and EGR/DPF issues. With proper maintenance and proactive attention to known issues, all can be reliable.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz E200 CDI / E200d?
items.answer: W211 (OM646): Injector seal leaks ("black death"), crankshaft position sensor failure, EGR valve clogging. W212 (OM651): Timing chain stretch (rattling on cold start — can destroy the engine if ignored), injector seal leaks, EGR/DPF clogging. W213 (OM654): Timing chain wear, injector seal leaks, EGR/DPF clogging, camshaft and rocker wear.

items.question: Is it worth replacing an E200 CDI / E200d engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on the vehicle value and engine era. Against a well‑kept W213 worth £15,000–£25,000, a £5,000–£6,000 recon is 20–30% of the car's value — worth it. Against a tired W211 worth £2,000–£4,000, the same recon may exceed our 70% Mercedes‑Benz threshold — get a scrap valuation first. The W213 OM654 is the most desirable due to its modern efficiency and refinement.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz E200 CDI / E200d engine code?
items.answer: W211 (2002–2009): OM646. W212 (2009–2016): OM651.925. W213 (2016–present): OM654. Check the VIN plate or vehicle documentation. The engine code is also stamped on the engine block.

items.question: Can I repair the OM651 timing chain, or does the engine need replacement?
items.answer: The timing chain and tensioner must be replaced if worn. A preventative replacement costs £1,500–£3,500. A snapped chain almost always destroys the engine, requiring a £3,200–£5,500+ replacement. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes are the key to preventing timing chain issues.

faq.disclaimer: All prices are indicative UK market ranges based on historical quote data from our network of engine specialists. Actual costs vary by variant, engine condition, damage severity and workshop location. Use our free comparison tool to get tailored quotes.
